name: Celestial devas
shortDescription: Celestial devas are divine beings in Buddhist cosmology, inhabiting higher realms and often associated with light, wisdom, and protection.
description: Celestial devas, also known as the gods or shining ones, are prominent figures in Buddhist cosmology, residing in the higher realms of existence within the Realm of Desire (Kāmadhātu) and the Realm of Form (Rūpadhātu). They are considered to be beings of great power, wisdom, and compassion, who are often associated with light, beauty, and spiritual attainment. While not immortal, devas possess exceptionally long lifespans and enjoy a life of relative ease and pleasure compared to beings in lower realms. They are not free from suffering (dukkha) and are still subject to the cycle of rebirth (samsara). Their elevated status is the result of accumulated positive karma from past lives. Some devas are protectors of the Dharma (Buddhist teachings), while others are venerated for their wisdom and guidance. They can interact with humans and other beings, offering assistance or posing challenges. Different schools and traditions of Buddhism may have varying classifications and descriptions of devas, but they generally represent beings who have cultivated virtue and attained a high level of spiritual development. The concept of celestial devas played a crucial role in the spread of Buddhism across various cultures, often adapting and merging with local deities and beliefs. Their iconography is frequently found in Buddhist art and architecture, such as in the carvings and sculptures of Angkor Wat.

sources.title: The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism
sources.author: Robert E. Buswell Jr. and Donald S. Lopez Jr.
